claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 15 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Thanks for your help. I'd like to claim for my three children. 

I have Alex who is 4 years old, Blake who is 16, and Charlie who is 15.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's lovely that you're looking to claim for Alex (4), Blake (16), and Charlie (15).

Now, could you please confirm: do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Next, could you tell me about your living arrangements with the children? Do all three children (Alex, Blake, and Charlie) live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex and Charlie live with me at home, but Blake doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Blake doesn't live with you, I have a couple of follow-up questions. Let me start with Blake:

Do you contribute towards Blake's upkeep (for example, by paying for food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing to their accommodation)? If so, is this contribution at least equal to the Child Benefit amount (currently £25.60 per week for the eldest child, or £16.95 for additional children)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards Blake's upkeep. I give about £50 a week to help with their expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you, that's helpful to know. Your contribution of £50 per week is well above the Child Benefit threshold, which is good.

Since Blake is 16, I need to ask: is Blake currently in approved education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, or certain vocational qualifications) or approved training (unpaid and not provided by an employer)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake recently left school and has just signed up with the local careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know – if Blake has registered with a government-sponsored careers service after leaving education, you may be able to continue receiving Child Benefit for Blake for up to 20 weeks.

Now I just have a couple more questions to complete the picture:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for any of these three children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just one more question to ensure I have all the relevant information:

Are any of the children (Alex, Blake, or Charlie) currently:
- In hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks, or
- In local authority care for more than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a bit – about 3 months now. But I still spend money on things for him like clothes and toys and his favourite snacks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I'm sorry to hear Alex has been in hospital for such a long time – that must be difficult for your family.

Since Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (approximately 12 weeks or more), but you mention you're still regularly spending money on him, this is relevant to your eligibility. 

Just to clarify: Is Alex in a hospital in the UK? And are you regularly spending money on Alex (it sounds like you are with the clothes, toys, and snacks)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's in a hospital here in the UK, and yes, I'm regularly spending money on him – buying clothes, toys, and his favourite snacks and things like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. It's good to know you're continuing to support Alex while he's in hospital.

I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for all three children. Let me provide you with my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm pleased to tell you that **you appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for all three of your children**. Here's a summary:

---

### **Alex (age 4)** ✅ Eligible
Although Alex has been in hospital for over 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on him (clothes, toys, snacks). The rules allow Child Benefit to continue for children in a UK hospital beyond the 12-week limit if you're regularly spending money on them.

---

### **Blake (age 16)** ✅ Eligible
Even though Blake has left school and doesn't live with you, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks because Blake has registered with a careers service. You also contribute £50 per week towards Blake's upkeep, which is well above the required threshold.

---

### **Charlie (age 15)** ✅ Eligible
Charlie is under 16 and lives with you, so you meet the standard eligibility criteria.

---

### Important Notes:
- **For Blake**: The eligibility through the careers service registration lasts for up to 20 weeks, so you'll want to keep track of Blake's plans after that period.
- **For Alex**: You should inform HMRC about Alex's hospital stay if you haven't already, and continue to keep records of your spending on him.
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of your children, so there are no conflicts.

I hope this helps! If you have any further questions, please don't hesitate to ask.

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old, which is under 16, and you live in the UK. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (over the 12-week threshold), you are regularly spending money on him (clothes, toys, snacks). The rules state that for children in a UK hospital, if you regularly spend money on them, you can continue to receive Child Benefit even after the 12-week period. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old. Normally, Child Benefit continues if a child aged 16-19 stays in approved education or training. Blake has left school but has registered with a local careers service. The rules allow you to apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with a government-sponsored careers service. Additionally, although Blake doesn't live with you, you contribute £50 per week towards their upkeep, which is well above the Child Benefit amount threshold. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 15 years old, which is under 16, and lives with you in the UK. You are responsible for Charlie as they live with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and are responsible for all three children. For Alex (age 4), despite being in hospital for over 12 weeks, you continue to regularly spend money on him, which allows eligibility to continue. For Blake (age 16), although they have left school and don't live with you, they have registered with a careers service (allowing up to 20 weeks of continued benefit) and you contribute significantly to their upkeep. For Charlie (age 15), they live with you and meet all standard eligibility criteria. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of these children.

For Charlie (age 15), they live with you and meet all standard eligibility criteria. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of these children.
